paragraphs.text:
Shang archaeology began as the archaeology of one place.
That place was Anyang in northern Henan, firmly linked to the Shang of traditional history by the oracle-bone inscriptions found there.

paragraphs.text:
As more sites were discovered after 1950, the field widened beyond Anyang.
Yet many of those newer sites had no inscriptions.
Their political status was therefore uncertain.

paragraphs.text:
Chinese archaeologists often solved that uncertainty by fitting the new finds into the old historical picture.
If later tradition described the Shang king as the dominant power of the age, then distant sites were treated as Shang dependents or as vaguely subordinate outsiders.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

paragraphs.text:
The opening objection is that this procedure assumes in advance what archaeology ought to discover.
Even the oracle texts from Anyang do not independently prove that the Shang court stood at the unquestioned center of all civilized life.
They too have usually been read through that same inherited model.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

paragraphs.text:
As discoveries multiplied, the weakness of the model became harder to ignore.
By the later second millennium BC, civilized societies existed across a broad area, and many of them were far from Anyang and unlike it in material culture.
To attach all of that diversity to a single royal house now looks forced.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.caption: The mature late Shang world centered at Anyang, archaeologically exceptional but no longer treated as the whole of civilization.

paragraphs.text:
The chapter therefore proposes a more cautious procedure.
It tries to describe the archaeological record without pretending that the final historical picture is already known.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

paragraphs.text:
For that reason, some terms are narrowed sharply.
Shang and Shang dynasty are to refer only to the ruling family named in the Anyang oracle texts.
Anyang period refers only to the time of the nine kings whose inscriptions have been found there, and only to the Anyang site itself.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.caption: The broad prehistoric field within which the chapter tries to orient the evidence without later dynastic assumptions.

timeline.label: Early Bronze Age

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The mature late Shang world centered at Anyang, archaeologically exceptional but no longer treated as the whole of civilization.

paragraphs.text:
Other terms are handled with equal caution.
Shang period is avoided because it has no secure archaeological definition.
Dynasty is dangerous because Chinese historiography loads it with meanings far beyond the simple idea of a ruling family.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The mature late Shang world centered at Anyang, archaeologically exceptional but no longer treated as the whole of civilization.

paragraphs.text:
Chinese civilization is also treated as a problematic phrase.
Projected backward into the second millennium BC, it becomes vague and anachronistic.
Used carelessly, it can make the archaeological past look more unified than it really was.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The mature late Shang world centered at Anyang, archaeologically exceptional but no longer treated as the whole of civilization.

paragraphs.text:
Apart from two or three centuries in the life of one city, the second millennium remains prehistory.
Later traditions cannot simply be used as a guide to it.
But the difficulty is that archaeology itself has often been shaped by those traditions, above all by the long focus on the Central Plain.

paragraphs.text:
The discussion therefore turns to a different anchor.
Chance finds, especially bronzes, provide a corrective to the geography imposed by texts.
They also offer a way to define the chapter's scope archaeologically rather than dynastically.

sections.title: Archaeology and the Crisis of Tradition

paragraphs.text:
From the beginning, Chinese archaeology was entangled with arguments about the truth of inherited history.
The fall of the Qing in 1911 shook confidence in traditional culture and forced intellectuals to reconsider what in the ancient record could still be trusted.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Fall of the Qing
timeline.caption: The political collapse that sharpened the crisis over what ancient tradition could still be trusted.

paragraphs.text:
The oracle bones entered this debate at exactly the right moment.
Collectors had begun acquiring them around the turn of the century, and scholars quickly saw that they contained names known from the received list of Shang kings.

figures.label: Map 3.1

timeline.caption: Collectors began acquiring oracle bones around the turn of the century, making them central to modern debates about antiquity.

paragraphs.text:
For some scholars, especially Luo Zhenyu and Wang Guowei, the inscriptions seemed a priceless vindication of tradition.
For Gu Jiegang they had a different value.
They did not confirm everything in the old narratives, but they did suggest that Shang stood on firmer ground than the Xia and the still earlier sage kings.

paragraphs.text:
That distinction mattered.
Gu had already argued that the stories of Xia and the ancient sages had much of the character of folklore.
The oracle bones therefore promised not a return to old certainty, but the possibility of a more rigorous history.

paragraphs.text:
Even the authenticity of the bones was contested for a time.
At least one prominent scholar denounced them as forgeries.
The excavations at Anyang would eventually settle that question.

sections.title: Anyang and the Birth of Field Archaeology

paragraphs.text:
By 1915 Luo Zhenyu had traced the source of the oracle bones to farmers near modern Anyang.
Map 3.1 helps place that region and the site that soon became decisive for early Chinese archaeology.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Early oracle-bone debates
timeline.caption: The formative decades in which oracle bones, textual criticism, and modern historical doubt first collided.

timeline.label: Anyang excavations
timeline.caption: The first sustained excavation campaign at Anyang, which turned oracle-bone collecting into field archaeology.

timeline.label: National archaeology after 1949
timeline.caption: The era in which archaeology became state-supported and increasingly tied to national historical narratives.

timeline.label: Oracle-bone source traced to Anyang
timeline.caption: The source of the oracle bones was traced to farmers near modern Anyang, linking the inscriptions to a real site.

paragraphs.text:
What transformed the matter was not epigraphy alone, but field archaeology.
That discipline arrived in China from the West and found a favorable climate in the reforming atmosphere of the May Fourth era.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Early oracle-bone debates
timeline.caption: The formative decades in which oracle bones, textual criticism, and modern historical doubt first collided.

timeline.label: May Fourth era
timeline.caption: The reforming climate in which Western scholarly methods, including archaeology, found unusual prestige in China.

timeline.label: Anyang excavations
timeline.caption: The first sustained excavation campaign at Anyang, which turned oracle-bone collecting into field archaeology.

paragraphs.text:
Its promise had already been demonstrated by the Swedish geologist J G Andersson, whose discoveries revealed the first Neolithic sites recognized in China.
When the Academia Sinica decided in 1928 to undertake an excavation, Anyang was the obvious place to begin.
Li Ji, trained as an anthropologist at Harvard, was chosen to direct the work.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Early oracle-bone debates
timeline.caption: The formative decades in which oracle bones, textual criticism, and modern historical doubt first collided.

timeline.label: May Fourth era
timeline.caption: The reforming climate in which Western scholarly methods, including archaeology, found unusual prestige in China.

timeline.label: Anyang excavations
timeline.caption: The first sustained excavation campaign at Anyang, which turned oracle-bone collecting into field archaeology.

paragraphs.text:
The result was larger than anyone first imagined.
Chinese archaeology was born from the meeting of a Western scientific method and a question charged with enormous significance for Chinese historical identity.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.caption: The formative decades in which oracle bones, textual criticism, and modern historical doubt first collided.

timeline.caption: The era in which archaeology became state-supported and increasingly tied to national historical narratives.

paragraphs.text:
Between 1928 and 1937, despite difficult local conditions, fifteen seasons of excavation were carried out at Anyang.
The work soon expanded beyond the search for inscribed bones.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Early oracle-bone debates
timeline.caption: The formative decades in which oracle bones, textual criticism, and modern historical doubt first collided.

timeline.label: Anyang excavations
timeline.caption: The first sustained excavation campaign at Anyang, which turned oracle-bone collecting into field archaeology.

timeline.label: National archaeology after 1949
timeline.caption: The era in which archaeology became state-supported and increasingly tied to national historical narratives.

paragraphs.text:
A temporary move to Chengziyai in Shandong proved especially important.
There the excavators found a distinctive black pottery culture and learned to recognize rammed earth construction, which they had earlier mistaken at Anyang for natural deposit.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Early oracle-bone debates
timeline.caption: The formative decades in which oracle bones, textual criticism, and modern historical doubt first collided.

timeline.label: Anyang excavations
timeline.caption: The first sustained excavation campaign at Anyang, which turned oracle-bone collecting into field archaeology.

timeline.label: National archaeology after 1949
timeline.caption: The era in which archaeology became state-supported and increasingly tied to national historical narratives.

paragraphs.text:
Back at Anyang they uncovered a stratified sequence at Hougang.
Painted pottery lay below, black pottery in the middle, and Shang remains above.
They took this to reveal a native eastern component in East Asian prehistory.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

paragraphs.text:
Later work at Xiaotun and Xibeigang made the scale of the site unmistakable.
There were great rammed-earth foundations, huge shaft tombs, sacrificial burials, and in 1936 a pit containing more than seventeen thousand fragments of inscribed turtle plastrons.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.caption: The formative decades in which oracle bones, textual criticism, and modern historical doubt first collided.

timeline.label: Anyang excavations
timeline.caption: The first sustained excavation campaign at Anyang, which turned oracle-bone collecting into field archaeology.

timeline.label: National archaeology after 1949
timeline.caption: The era in which archaeology became state-supported and increasingly tied to national historical narratives.

timeline.label: Xibeigang cemetery identified
timeline.caption: Discovery of the great cemetery at Xibeigang revealed the scale of royal mortuary archaeology at Anyang.

timeline.label: Xiaotun oracle-bone pit
timeline.caption: A pit with more than seventeen thousand inscribed plastron fragments confirmed the scale of the Anyang archive.

paragraphs.text:
The excavations began as an attempt to locate the source of the oracle bones.
They ended by uncovering an entire civilization.
That double success reassured Chinese scholars that tradition might be trustworthy, and persuaded Western scholars that China possessed a truly ancient past.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Early oracle-bone debates
timeline.caption: The formative decades in which oracle bones, textual criticism, and modern historical doubt first collided.

timeline.label: Anyang excavations
timeline.caption: The first sustained excavation campaign at Anyang, which turned oracle-bone collecting into field archaeology.

timeline.label: National archaeology after 1949
timeline.caption: The era in which archaeology became state-supported and increasingly tied to national historical narratives.

paragraphs.text:
But the triumph had a price.
Because Anyang seemed to vindicate the texts, it encouraged the belief that archaeology as a whole should confirm the traditional dynastic narrative.
A city and a few centuries came to stand for far more than the evidence could strictly bear.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

paragraphs.text:
Anyang gave archaeology a secure place in modern China.
A discipline that might have been dismissed as foreign instead won state support under both Republican and Communist governments.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Anyang excavations
timeline.caption: The first sustained excavation campaign at Anyang, which turned oracle-bone collecting into field archaeology.

timeline.label: National archaeology after 1949
timeline.caption: The era in which archaeology became state-supported and increasingly tied to national historical narratives.

timeline.label: Salvage archaeology
timeline.caption: Rescue work and chance finds widened the archaeological sample far beyond sites chosen chiefly from texts.

paragraphs.text:
Yet the mission of the field was narrowed.
The archaeology of the Early Bronze Age remained largely the archaeology of the Shang dynasty, guided by images inherited from late Zhou and Han texts rather than from archaeology itself.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

paragraphs.text:
Li Ji had been willing to consider diffusion from western Asia.
He thought Chinese metallurgy, some artifact types, and even the sudden appearance of major bronze vessels might owe something to outside stimulus.
For an archaeologist of his generation, diffusion was a natural explanation for culture change.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

paragraphs.text:
After 1949, however, the dominant demand was different.
Ancient China had to be shown as indigenous and self-developing.
When excavations at Zhengzhou revealed a great walled city earlier than Anyang, and Erlitou later supplied a still earlier stage, the need to invoke foreign origins largely disappeared.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.caption: The era in which archaeology became state-supported and increasingly tied to national historical narratives.

timeline.label: Salvage archaeology
timeline.caption: Rescue work and chance finds widened the archaeological sample far beyond sites chosen chiefly from texts.

timeline.label: Zhengzhou excavations begin
timeline.caption: Excavation of the great earlier walled city at Zhengzhou weakened the need to explain Anyang by foreign stimulus.

timeline.label: Erlitou discovered
timeline.caption: Discovery of Erlitou supplied a still earlier stage before Zhengzhou in the middle Yellow River sequence.

paragraphs.text:
The result was not the rejection of diffusion in general.
It was the rejection of foreign influence into the Central Plain, while influence radiating outward from the Yellow River valley remained easy to assume.
That kept the old centralizing pattern intact in a new form.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

sections.title: Chance Finds Beyond the Central Plain

paragraphs.text:
The real corrective came from salvage archaeology and chance finds.
After 1950 the state encouraged the reporting of objects found during farming and construction.
This created a much wider archaeological sample than the one produced by text-guided excavation alone.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Anyang excavations
timeline.caption: The first sustained excavation campaign at Anyang, which turned oracle-bone collecting into field archaeology.

timeline.label: National archaeology after 1949
timeline.caption: The era in which archaeology became state-supported and increasingly tied to national historical narratives.

timeline.label: Salvage archaeology
timeline.caption: Rescue work and chance finds widened the archaeological sample far beyond sites chosen chiefly from texts.

timeline.label: Archaeological expansion beyond Anyang
timeline.caption: After 1950, newly reported sites without inscriptions forced the field beyond its original Anyang focus.

paragraphs.text:
One early shock was Panlongcheng in Hubei, an Erligang-period walled city far south of Zhengzhou and culturally very close to it.
Its discovery showed that the Erligang world extended much farther than many had imagined.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.caption: The formative horizon in which large-scale casting, elite display, and early urban organization first come together.

timeline.caption: The first great bronze-casting horizon of the Central Plain, marked by standardized vessels and rapid expansion.

timeline.label: Transition period
timeline.caption: The century in which the short-lived unity of Erligang gives way to clearer regional bronze traditions.

timeline.label: Erligang expansion
timeline.caption: A major outward expansion from the middle Yellow River region is the simplest explanation for the wide Erligang horizon.

paragraphs.text:
Further south, the evidence became harder to absorb into the old picture.
Bronzes from the middle and lower Yangzi region were often highly distinctive.
For a long time they were still explained away as refugees from the north, peoples named in oracle texts, or groups labeled with later ethnonyms.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The mature late Shang world centered at Anyang, archaeologically exceptional but no longer treated as the whole of civilization.

paragraphs.text:
Only slowly did archaeologists begin to insist on local development in places such as Jiangxi and Hunan.
The discoveries at Sanxingdui in Sichuan and at Xin'gan in Jiangxi finally made the old explanations much harder to maintain.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Sanxingdui pits discovered

timeline.label: Xin'gan tomb discovered
timeline.caption: The rich tomb at Xin'gan made southern bronze traditions much harder to dismiss as peripheral.

paragraphs.text:
Hence the newer talk of multiple centers of innovation.
The widening of attention is accepted, but the phrase still smuggles in a later goal, as though all these cultures mattered chiefly because they contributed to some future Chinese civilization.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

paragraphs.text:
His deeper point is simple.
The real subject should be the rise of civilization in China, not the back projection of a later civilizational unity.
If archaeology is read too eagerly through the lens of later China, diversity and contingency disappear.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

sections.title: Defining the Bronze Age in China

paragraphs.text:
To escape dynastic geography, the discussion turns to metallurgy.
The bronze industry, by its scale and character, offers an archaeological way to identify the first civilized societies in China.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: First half of the second millennium BC
timeline.caption: The long formative span before the mature Erligang and Anyang worlds.

timeline.label: Early Bronze Age
timeline.caption: The age in which large-scale casting and elite bronze cultures define the first clearly civilized societies in China.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The mature late Shang world centered at Anyang, archaeologically exceptional but no longer treated as the whole of civilization.

paragraphs.text:
In many parts of the world the term Bronze Age is only a convenient label.
The first appearance of copper or bronze does not automatically coincide with the rise of cities or stratified societies.
Bronze tools were not everywhere the motor of civilization.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

paragraphs.text:
China is different.
Its early bronze industry reached an extraordinary scale.
The famous Si Mu Wu fangding alone weighs about eight hundred and seventy five kilograms, and one royal tomb at Anyang contained about sixteen hundred kilograms of bronze.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Early Bronze Age

paragraphs.text:
That scale implies labor, organization, mining, fuel, transport, workshops, and elite command.
Whether or not bronze caused the rise of civilization, it provides a clear index of social complexity.
In China, the term Bronze Age is therefore genuinely meaningful.

paragraphs.text:
An important distinction is then drawn.
There is a world of difference between small-scale metallurgy and large-scale metallurgy.
The first involves technical knowledge used for modest tools and ornaments.
The second involves specialists, heavy patronage, artistic distinction, and production on a new scale.

For this chapter, large-scale casting and the technical repertoire associated with it define the Early Bronze Age.
They also define the field of inquiry more securely than any attempt to map later dynasties back onto the second millennium BC.

sections.title: Small-Scale Metallurgy Before the Bronze Age

paragraphs.text:
The earliest clear evidence for metallurgy in China comes from the Qijia culture in Gansu, around 2000 BC.
Here one finds a genuinely early stage of metal use.

Qijia metalwork includes mirrors, finger rings, pendants, knives, awls, chisels, and axes.
Some pieces were hammered.
Some were cast in simple molds.
Most seem to be copper, sometimes alloyed with lead or tin.

A richer successor, the Huoshaogou site at Yumen, also belongs to the first half of the second millennium BC.
Its graves yielded more than two hundred metal artifacts, including axes, sickles, daggers, spearheads, needles, bracelets, hammers, and mirrors.

What matters is not only the presence of metal, but its social character.
These are still small tools and ornaments, not highly distinctive prestige objects made for a ruling elite.
This is metallurgy before the urban revolution shows itself clearly in the archaeological record.

That question is left open.
But it offers a useful contrast, because it shows what metalworking looks like before the great explosion of scale seen at Erlitou and Erligang.

sections.title: Erlitou and the Turn to Large-Scale Casting

paragraphs.text:
At Erlitou something changes decisively.
In the upper levels of the site one finds bronze vessels, bells, and weapons more ambitious than anything yet known from Qijia.

paragraphs.text:
The importance of Erlitou lies not only in quantity, though the foundry remains are said to cover a vast area.
It lies above all in method.
The vessels were cast.

paragraphs.text:
In most other ancient metal traditions, vessels and similar shapes were normally hammered, which conserved metal.
At Erlitou one sees the first unmistakable sign of a characteristically Chinese extravagance in bronze production.

paragraphs.text:
That extravagance presupposed two things at once.
There had to be geological abundance of ore.
There also had to be the social ability to mobilize labor for mining, transport, fuel gathering, and workshop production.

paragraphs.text:
This is why bronze matters so much in the argument.
The leap from Qijia to Erlitou is not chiefly a leap in technical knowledge.
It is a leap in organization, scale, and social purpose.
The bronzes of Erlitou are traces of the urban revolution in material form.

paragraphs.text:
The next stage, Erligang, transformed this new metallurgy into a mature technical system.
Bronze vessels now become abundant, and the industry makes advances that would shape Chinese casting for centuries.

paragraphs.text:
A crucial step was the use of section molds in increasingly flexible ways.
Figure 3.1 makes the process unusually clear.
It shows how a clay core and outer mold sections worked together in casting a tripod vessel.

paragraphs.text:
Because the mold could be opened before casting, decoration could be carved into its inner surface.
That meant decoration was cast, not cut afterward into cold metal.
This technical choice had long artistic consequences.

paragraphs.text:
Erligang casters also learned to cast objects in stages.
Parts could be made separately, then embedded in a later mold and locked into place when the next pour was made.
They also used bronze spacers to hold cores and mold walls at the right distance during casting.

These procedures sound simple when described in words.
In practice they created a highly versatile system.
By the end of the Erligang period, the essential technical repertoire for complex bronze casting was already in place.

The artistic achievement of Erligang was not decoration alone.
It was the deep fit between decoration and shape.
The chapter argues that this relation grew directly from the logic of mold making.

paragraphs.text:
When decoration is carved section by section into the mold, each section naturally invites a complete and self-contained pattern.
The divisions required by casting therefore leave their trace on the finished bronze.
The vessel's shape determines the mold.
The mold in turn helps determine the layout of the decoration.

Over time this produced a powerful visual order.
Patterns grew more elaborate, but the subdivisions of the vessel remained legible.
The result was a close marriage of form and ornament that would dominate bronze design for centuries.

Figure 3.2 shows the point in a relatively early and simple form.
The bronze ding from Panlongcheng still carries a pattern whose repetition follows the logic of the mold sections and the placement of the legs.

Later casters could have abandoned this way of organizing decoration, but they did not.
Even after they moved from carving on mold sections to carving on the model, they preserved the subdivided logic.
Only much later, toward the end of Western Zhou, did that old discipline begin to loosen.

paragraphs.text:
A developmental sequence of five styles was set out in 1953 for pre-Zhou bronze decoration.
Later excavations showed that the sequence began earlier than first supposed, with the first decorated bronzes at Erlitou and the fully mature fifth style by about 1200 BC.

What gives the sequence its lasting value is not merely chronology.
It captures an internal logic of development.
It shows how one solution in design and casting opened the way to the next.

The sequence is therefore more than a classificatory grid.
It helps explain why certain forms appear before others, and how the bronze industry expanded its visual repertoire over time.
It also helps make sense of regional branching after the Erligang age.

These five styles should not be imagined as rigid period boxes.
Each survived after later ones appeared.
They are better understood as stages in the growth of a repertoire that continued to accumulate rather than simply replacing itself.

sections.title: Style I and the First Decorative Logic

paragraphs.text:
Style I is the thread relief seen on the early ding of the Erligang world, as in Figure 3.2.
Its thin raised lines stand proud on the bronze because the design was carved directly into the mold sections.

The mold was divided according to the practical needs of manufacture.
The decoration then occupied those same divided fields.

The result was a close relation between vessel shape, mold structure, and ornament.
The repeated design follows the same rhythm as the vessel itself.
On a tripod, for example, the units of pattern fall in step with the placement of the legs.

The best known unit in this early repertoire is the two-eyed animal-like motif conventionally called taotie.
At this stage it is not yet a dramatic image separated from its ground.
It is a compact patterned form, repeated within the fixed compartments created by the casting process.

sections.title: From Style II to Style III

paragraphs.text:
Style II begins when those thin lines cease to be uniform.
The raised bands broaden and narrow, giving the designs a more deliberate and expressive contour.
A profile animal, reasonably described as a dragon, joins the taotie as a standard motif.

paragraphs.text:
Style III grows out of this by elaboration.
The patterns become denser, more intricate, and more expansive.
They spread across larger surfaces and diversify into many local variants.

By this stage the decoration was no longer being cut into the mold itself.
It was cut on the model from which the mold was taken.
That shift removed the technical necessity for subdivision, yet the subdivided layout was preserved because it had become visually compelling.

paragraphs.text:
Late Style III also introduced experiments in high relief.
The broad undulating surfaces seen on vessels such as Figure 3.6 gave animal forms more force after the older fine-line meshes had become hard to read.
This fuller relief matters because it anticipates later developments without yet becoming the same thing.

paragraphs.text:
Style IV marks a decisive change.
The old dense patterning is reorganized so that motif and ground pull apart.
The animal image now stands out clearly against a tightly worked background.

That background is often made of tiny spirals conventionally called leiwen.
Earlier pattern meshes are reduced and miniaturized until they become a field rather than an image.
The contrast gives the vessel a new visual authority.

This also changes the status of the dragon and the taotie.
In the earlier styles they were patterned units with animal suggestions.
In Style IV they become more definite creatures, sharply bounded and visually insistent.

Style V pushes the effect further by raising the motifs above the ground in high relief.
At the same time, vertical flanges often reinforce the divisions of the vessel.
The whole object becomes more theatrical, more emphatic, and more commanding in its visual presence.

The later one has the air of a fully staged court style.

The bronzes become richer because the foundries are thinking more consciously about visual effect.

The bronzes therefore become unusually good historical evidence.
Architecture survives poorly.
Texts are late and selective.
But bronzes preserve fine differences of time, place, and affiliation with exceptional clarity.

They also travel.
As gifts, prestige objects, or models for imitation, they can link one court to another.
For that reason the history of bronze style becomes one of the best guides to the wider history of the Early Bronze Age.

sections.title: An Archaeological Reading of Early Bronze Age History

paragraphs.text:
The archaeological record suggests a broad sequence of events.
Urban societies first arose in the middle Yellow River region during the first half of the second millennium BC.
Then, around 1500 BC, a major state seems to have expanded outward from that region.

paragraphs.text:
The clearest material expression of that expansion is the wide and short-lived spread of the Erligang style.
Because the bronzes are so uniform across a large area, they look less like loose cultural resemblance than like the reach of an organized power.
In that sense the Erligang style behaves like a horizon style.

Local bronze traditions began to assert themselves on the frontiers.
The transition period, roughly corresponding to the thirteenth century BC, is the moment when regional styles become unmistakable.

Those local industries still depend on Erligang foundations.
They share its casting methods, and many still make bronze vessels.
But they also adapt the technology to local needs, producing forms such as large bells and drums in the Yangzi region.

The world that emerges is not one of a single unquestioned center.
It is a network of interacting powers, among them the dynasty later known from Anyang.
Some of its neighbors were culturally different, and some were by no means inferior.

sections.title: Erlitou and the First Urban Horizon

paragraphs.text:
Against that larger background, Erlitou appears as the first clearly urban horizon in the story.
Its importance lies not only in a few striking objects, but in the appearance together of monumental building, ambitious burial, and cast bronze vessels.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

paragraphs.text:
The site was discovered near Luoyang in western Henan and gave its name to a wider archaeological culture extending across much of the surrounding region.
The mixture of older and newer traits is striking.
Some elements are still recognizably Neolithic.
Others open directly into the Bronze Age proper.

paragraphs.text:
Among the features already present are pounded-earth construction, rich burials, cast bronze vessels, turquoise inlay, lacquered wood, divination by animal bones, human sacrifice, and the ge weapon in bronze and jade.
This is not yet the fully developed world of Zhengzhou or Anyang.
But it is already a society of rank, labor mobilization, and emerging court culture.

Much of what appears at Erlitou survives for centuries afterward.
It is one of those sites where later Chinese Bronze Age civilization can be seen forming before it has reached its full scale.

Erlitou lies on the south bank of the Luo River and extends over a large area.
In its central zone, archaeologists found pounded-earth terraces and building foundations that imply planned monumental architecture.
Figure 3.11 gives a sense of one of the main compounds.

The largest excavated terrace is a great square platform more than one hundred meters across.
It was built by pounding thin layers of earth into an excavation and then raising the surface above the surrounding ground.
This was laborious work, and its scale is already socially revealing.

Inside the enclosure stood a large pillared hall.
Post holes suggest a rectangular timber building with an unroofed porch along the south side.
If smaller outer holes belonged to eave supports, the roof may even have been hipped.

A pillared hall inside a walled compound, built on pounded earth, is the beginning of a durable tradition.

sections.title: Tombs and Social Rank at Erlitou

paragraphs.text:
One of the most revealing Erlitou compounds also contained a large tomb dug into the terrace.
Its shaft was rectangular, deep, and furnished with an ercengtai, the ledge or shelf left around the coffin chamber.
The grave had been plundered, but its scale still speaks plainly.

The tomb also contained traces of cinnabar, fragments of a lacquer coffin, and the skeleton of a dog that seems to have had a lacquer coffin of its own.
Even stripped of its goods, the burial announces a rank far above that of ordinary graves.
Its size is comparable to one of the most famous royal tombs later found at Anyang.

Other rich Erlitou burials are smaller, but they repeat a recognizable set of signs.
There are traces of lacquer, cinnabar, the ercengtai, and often a waist pit beneath the coffin.
Bronze, jade, and pottery grave goods complete the pattern.

paragraphs.text:
What emerges is social hierarchy rather than a clearly named political system.
The foundations and tombs prove organized labor and strong distinctions of rank.
But archaeology still cannot say with confidence what sort of state, if any, stood behind them.

The bronzes from Erlitou are the clearest evidence yet of a transformed metal industry in early second-millennium China.
The alloy figures remain limited, so the term bronze is used cautiously.
But the analyzed pieces contain enough tin to suggest deliberate alloying.

The inventory includes vessels, bells, plaques, knives, and ge blades, as seen in Figures 3.12 and 3.13.
One ring-handled knife may even point northward, suggesting contact with the Northern Zone.

paragraphs.text:
The pattern seen in Jiangxi seems not to have been unique.
After the short-lived Erligang expansion, local bronze-using cultures emerged in several regions.
What broke down was uniformity, not contact.

The transition period was therefore not a time of isolation.
It was a time when local styles began to form inside a larger field of interaction.

paragraphs.text:
The most tantalizing transition period center may be Chenggu in southern Shaanxi, at the meeting of the Han River and the Xushui.
Repeated accidental finds there have produced more than five hundred bronzes, mostly weapons, but also chariot fittings and fine vessels.

The oracle inscriptions found there were made on behalf of nine kings whose names later tradition preserves as the last rulers of the Shang or Yin dynasty.

There are inscribed oracle bones, huge tombs, chariot burials, and human sacrifice on a scale not seen earlier.

Its prominence reflects ancient reality, yet it also reflects decades of excavation.
If places such as Zhengzhou, Sanxingdui, or the Yangzi centers were equally well known, the balance of the picture might look different.

Writing is one reason Anyang looms so large.
Once written documents appear, the past instantly seems more concrete.
But this concreteness can mislead as much as it helps.

One question is whether writing itself first appeared at Anyang around 1200 BC.
Inscriptions show up on bronzes at about the same time that they appear on oracle bones, and that might suggest a true beginning.
Yet it is equally possible that writing had already existed on perishable materials.

The evidence from Anyang itself points in that direction.
There are signs of brush writing, and indirect evidence for wood or bamboo writing surfaces.
If so, the inscribed bones are only one surviving corner of a much larger written world.

Comparison with the Western Zhou makes the point clearer.
Without inscribed bronzes, Zhou literacy would hardly be visible archaeologically, even though it was surely much broader than the bronze record alone suggests.
The poverty of the Chinese textual record, compared with the clay tablets of the ancient Near East, may say more about preservation than about civilization.

They are the earliest surviving documents, but all come from one place and one narrow sphere.

Under Wu Ding the settlement expanded rapidly.

These foundations stand among pits used for storage, dwelling, sacrifice, and the keeping of oracle bones.
One pit held more than seventeen thousand turtle plastron fragments.

Its basic scheme recalls the much smaller elite tomb at Panlongcheng.

Its floor area was about 78 square meters, and its interior seems once to have been richly decorated.

Looters removed almost everything, yet enough remained to suggest a sumptuously furnished room filled with royal possessions.
The chamber was not just a container, but a constructed setting for the dead ruler.

Once the nine guardians had been buried in the floor, the space around the chamber was packed with pounded earth.

Taken together, the victims around M1001 seem to fall into two broad classes.
Some were buried carefully, perhaps as servants or close retainers.
Others were mutilated and treated as a more distant sacrificial class.

The closing of such a tomb must have been a prolonged public rite involving labor, display, and political meaning.
To stage a funeral on this scale was also to proclaim legitimacy.
The evidence from Xibeigang suggests that sacrifice to royal predecessors did not end with burial, but continued as part of the ongoing life of the dynasty.

More than 1,200 pits were excavated there in 1934 and 1935, most of them near M1400 in the eastern part of the cemetery.
Figure 3.20 helps one see the overall arrangement, with the great tombs at the center and the sacrificial zones spread around them.

The contents of the pits varied.
Complete skeletons more often had grave goods than dismembered ones, but even headless skeletons might be accompanied by knives, axes, or whetstones.
Some pits held horses, some birds, some monkeys, and in two striking cases elephants buried with human attendants.
The human pits were arranged in east west rows and in distinct groups, and each group tended to contain one consistent mode of killing and burial, as though it represented a single sacrificial occasion.

Later excavation made the scale clearer still.
In 1976, 191 pits southeast of M1400 were found to contain the remains of more than 1,200 victims.
Most were young adult males, though women and children also appeared.
Beheading was normal, but some victims were cut in half, dismembered, or in a few cases apparently buried alive.
The excavators distinguished twenty-two separate sacrificial groups, from single-pit offerings up to one immense group of forty-seven pits with more than 339 victims.

The western part of the cemetery yielded another pattern.
Forty pits excavated in 1978 seem to represent slightly later sacrifices, concentrated mainly on horses.
Taken together with test probes in the intervening ground, the evidence suggests that Xibeigang contained a continuous sacrificial zone used over a substantial span of time.
Oracle inscriptions from the reign of Wu Ding record offerings of human victims, sometimes by the hundred, dedicated to royal ancestors.
Xibeigang was almost certainly one of the main places where such rites were carried out.

sections.title: The Meaning of Sacrifice at Anyang

paragraphs.text:
Human sacrifice at Anyang did not serve only one function.
Foundation burials show that construction itself could demand victims, and sacrificial pits of uncertain purpose occur in many parts of the site.
Even so, the strongest concentration of evidence points to the mortuary cult of the kings.

figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

paragraphs.text:
What that cult meant to the wider population cannot be known directly.
A comparison with Mesoamerica is useful here, not because the two worlds were the same, but because it shows how large public sacrifices might combine political terror with religious necessity.
At Anyang too, sacrifice on this scale probably presupposed more than brute force alone.
It suggests a world in which the royal ancestors were believed to possess real power over the living.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

paragraphs.text:
The practice also marks the Anyang dynasty off from what came later.
Burial of retainers and funerary sacrifice survived in other forms, but the repeated slaughter of anonymous victims in great numbers after the funeral seems to have ended with Anyang.
Even the memory of it faded.
Western Zhou texts accuse the Shang kings of drunkenness rather than bloodiness, and later moralists seem strangely unaware that human sacrifice stood near the center of the old royal cult.

paragraphs.text:
The tomb of Fu Hao is the only intact royal burial yet discovered at Anyang, and also the only one that can be confidently tied to a named person in the oracle texts.
It lies west of the Xiaotun palace area and near several lesser burials of similar date.
It is far smaller than the great tombs of Xibeigang and has no entrance ramps, yet its contents are astonishing.
Figure 3.24 makes the plan clear.

paragraphs.text:
The shaft measured only about 4 by 5.6 meters at the mouth and 7.5 meters in depth.
Yet it contained roughly 1,600 kilograms of bronze and the largest group of jades ever found in an Anyang tomb.
The inventory included 195 bronze vessels, 271 other bronzes such as tools and weapons, 755 jades, 110 objects of marble, turquoise, and other stones, 564 carved bone objects, 15 shell pieces probably used as inlays, 3 ivory cups, and 11 pottery vessels.
If the plundered royal tombs at Xibeigang were richer still, their contents must have been almost beyond measure.

paragraphs.text:
The excavators reconstructed the bottom of the tomb as a set of nested lacquered coffins nearly filling the shaft.
The ercengtai ledge was very narrow, and most of the furnishings had been placed within the coffins themselves.
The largest bronzes lay in the outer coffin around three sides of the inner one, while the jades were placed with the dead in the inner coffin together with nearly 7,000 cowry shells.
Traces of cloth preserved in corrosion suggest that many bronzes had been wrapped before burial.

paragraphs.text:
The tomb also contained sacrificial victims.
Eight human skeletons, some incomplete, were found in the outer coffin.
Another lay with a dog in the yaokeng beneath, four more above the coffins, and three in niches cut into the sides of the shaft.
Even here, where the burial was vastly smaller than M1001, the royal funeral remained inseparable from sacrifice.

paragraphs.text:
The identification of the tomb depends mainly on inscriptions.
More than a hundred bronzes carry the name Fu Hao, and five others, including two monumental fangding, carry the name Mu Xin, which the oracle texts identify as the posthumous title under which Fu Hao received sacrifices from her sons.
That combination makes the attribution compelling.

paragraphs.text:
The inscriptions also raise a more delicate question.
Some scholars have thought that so many inscribed vessels could hardly have been made for the funeral alone, and therefore treated those bearing Fu Hao's name as possessions she had owned in life.
But this may underestimate the productive power of Anyang foundries.
A plausible alternative is that Wu Ding commissioned many of these vessels for the burial itself, while the great fangding to Mu Xin represent offerings made in her posthumous identity.

paragraphs.text:
The issue matters because it affects how the tomb is read.
If many bronzes were already in use, the tomb preserves something like a courtly household gathered into death.
If many were newly cast, it reveals the speed and scale with which royal ritual wealth could be mobilized.
In either case, the inscriptions show an unusually direct relation between archaeology and named historical persons.

paragraphs.text:
The bronze assemblage expresses rank in several different ways.
Some vessel types are themselves exceptional, appearing rarely or not at all in lesser burials.
Two fanglei and four fangyi belong to this category, along with a few singular pieces whose very oddity marks them out as aristocratic luxuries rather than basic ritual equipment.

paragraphs.text:
A second sign of status is sheer size.
Many vessels in the tomb are much larger than normal for their types.
The two largest, both fangding dedicated to Mu Xin, weigh about 120 kilograms each.
Rectangular versions of normally round forms are especially imposing.
There are only twenty-one rectangular vessels in the tomb, but they visually dominate the assemblage.

paragraphs.text:
A third sign of rank is quantity.
Forty jue and fifty-three gu are not just a ritual set but a display of abundance.
Where a lesser aristocrat might have one example, Fu Hao often had matched groups of five or ten.
A neighboring tomb like M18 would count as rich in almost any other context, yet beside Fu Hao's burial it seems modest and almost restrained.

paragraphs.text:
The distribution of forms also hints at ritual emphasis.
A rough functional division suggests that the majority of the vessels were for wine rather than food.
Almost all the major bronzes carry Style V decoration.
The result is a burial that speaks not only of wealth, but of ceremonial intensity and visual authority.

paragraphs.text:
Outside the vessel set, the bronzes include a dense cluster of tools and weapons.
There are eighty-nine ge blades and two especially large axe blades inscribed with Fu Hao's name.
Those axes may have served in sacrificial killing.
The tomb also yielded twenty-three small bells of the nao and ling types, a little horse gear, and several objects that seem to come from the Northern Zone, including mirrors, a knife, a pointed tool, and a number of so-called bow-shaped objects.
Figure 3.25 gathers these northern-looking pieces together.

figures.label: Figure 3.25
figures.caption: Northern-style bronzes from Fu Hao's tomb. They matter because they show that Anyang was in active contact with northern exchange networks.

paragraphs.text:
These items matter because they place Anyang within a wider network of exchange.
Mirrors of the same sort occur further northwest, even in contexts linked with Qijia and related northern traditions.
Other artifacts of unmistakably Anyang character turned up far away, as at Chenggu in Shaanxi.
The pattern suggests active traffic rather than cultural isolation.
By the time of Wu Ding, Anyang was not merely receiving influence from the north.
It was participating in a many-sided web of prestige exchange.

paragraphs.text:
The jades from Fu Hao's tomb require a different way of looking.
Jade is difficult to work.
It cannot simply be carved like wood or bone, but must be cut and shaped through abrasion.
That resistance is visible in the most unusual pieces from the tomb, especially the small three-dimensional animals, whose forms still seem half imprisoned in the rectangular blocks from which they were made.
Figure 3.26 shows this vividly.

figures.label: Figure 3.26
figures.caption: Three-dimensional jade animals from Fu Hao's tomb. The important point is how stubborn material and limited technique still produced vivid but compact forms.

paragraphs.text:
Flat objects were better suited to the material and to the available techniques.
For much of the Bronze Age, plaques, blades, disks, rings, and other flat forms are more numerous and often more refined than fully modeled figures.
The first step was to saw a pebble or block into slabs.
An object then had to be designed on the surface of the slab in such a way as to exploit attractive coloration, avoid flaws, and waste as little as possible.
That economy of material helped determine form.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

paragraphs.text:
The piece was then cut out, pierced, incised, and polished.
Saws, drills, and probably small rotating disks were the main tools.
Many jades in the tomb were clearly recut from earlier objects, showing both the value of the material and the way designs could emerge from the possibilities of a particular surviving fragment.
Some paired items were even cut from the same original piece.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

paragraphs.text:
The technical limitations had artistic consequences.
What emerged was largely a two-dimensional art, one that emphasized silhouette, polished surface, and the visual character of the stone itself.
Even when animals were represented, they were often treated as surfaces and outlines rather than as free sculptural masses.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

paragraphs.text:
Not all of Fu Hao's jades are decorated.
Some ge blades, disks, rings, and bracelets derive their effect simply from shape, polish, and material.
Where decoration does appear, it often takes the form of jagged sawn edges or fine linear patterns.
Those lines only seem to stand proud in relief.
In reality the craftsman often created the effect by grinding trenches on either side of the desired line.
The result is careful and elegant, though less fluid than the true thread relief that appears in some earlier Neolithic work.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

paragraphs.text:
The assemblage also contains several surprises.
One is the absence of large trapezoidal knives and arc-ended blades, shapes known from Erlitou and found in abundance at Sanxingdui.
Instead, the large blades in the tomb are almost all ge.
Thirty-nine of them were buried there.
Whether they were prized for ritual efficacy, prestige, or simple acquisitiveness cannot be said with certainty, but their concentration is striking.

paragraphs.text:
Another surprise is the breadth of the tomb's connections in time and space.
Some jades are probably imports from the south.
One ge even carries an inscription recording that a set of five ge had been sent in, a small but vivid trace of elite exchange.
More startling still is the presence of much older Neolithic pieces from several different cultures, including Shijiahe, Liangzhu, Longshan, and Hongshan.
Hongshan forms were evidently familiar enough at Anyang to inspire local adaptations, and Figure 3.27 shows how a foreign coiled animal type could gradually become an Anyang dragon plaque.

sections.title: Jades and Bronzes in Different Roles

paragraphs.text:
The contrast between the jades and the bronzes is revealing.
The jades include imports, heirlooms, and pieces already ancient when they entered the tomb.
Many were probably ornaments or cherished valuables in life before they became grave goods in death.
Some may even have been recovered from older burials.

paragraphs.text:
The bronzes tell a different story.
They belong much more consistently to the current ritual world of Anyang.
That makes sense.
A funerary assemblage of bronze vessels had to perform specific ceremonial work, and that required a coherent and up-to-date equipment set.
A random accumulation of old curiosities would not do.
The jades suggest personal possession and long circulation.
The bronzes suggest formal ritual order.

paragraphs.text:
The horse-drawn chariot appears at Anyang about the time of Wu Ding.
The earliest archaeological traces belong to that horizon, and there is no sign of any earlier wheeled vehicle in the area.
The graph for chariot in the oracle inscriptions of this period therefore becomes especially significant.
It almost certainly refers to a real vehicle already present in the life of the court.

paragraphs.text:
The striking thing is that the chariot enters the Chinese archaeological record fully formed.
There is no visible prehistory of wagons, carts, or draft use leading up to it.
The vehicle appears instead as part of a complete technical package, one that already had a long history in western Asia.
That makes the chariot an important clue to the external relations of the Anyang civilization.

paragraphs.text:
Chariot burials are known from several parts of the site.
Some were connected with tombs, others seem to have been separate sacrifices.
About twenty had been excavated when this discussion was written.
Most contained one or two chariots, a pair of horses for each vehicle, and in about half the cases one to three human skeletons.
The only contemporary example known elsewhere, at Laoniupo near Xi'an, is essentially identical to the Anyang specimens.

paragraphs.text:
Because early examples are often poorly preserved, one later burial is especially useful.
Pit M52 at Guojiazhuang, dated to Yinxu 4, preserved a nearly complete ghost of a chariot as a soil cast formed after the decay of the wood.
Figure 3.28 shows the arrangement clearly.
Two men and two horses had been laid in the pit before the vehicle was lowered into place.

figures.label: Figure 3.28
figures.caption: Chariot burial M52 at Anyang. The image makes the funerary arrangement of vehicle, horses, and attendants immediately visible.

paragraphs.text:
The pit was about 3.5 meters square and 1.8 meters deep.
The wheels, axle, and draft pole rested in trenches cut to receive them, so that the body of the chariot could sit low and the wheels would not bear the full weight of the fill.
Even so, the pounded earth later distorted the wheels and bent the draft pole.
One of the sacrificed men lay beside the horses on matting, his hands tied behind him and part of his body covered with patterned red cloth.
The other lay behind the vehicle and had been sprinkled with cinnabar.
The horses too were associated with cinnabar.

paragraphs.text:
Structurally the chariot was large but standard.
It had two eighteen-spoked wheels turning on a fixed axle, a draft pole resting over the axle and rising toward the yoke, and a curved yoke fitted with saddles resting on the horses' necks.
The axle was about 308 centimeters long and the distance between the wheels about 230 centimeters.
The box itself was broad enough to carry several passengers kneeling.

paragraphs.text:
The chariot box measured roughly a meter and a half across and just under a meter from front to back.
Its sides were formed by a low wooden lattice, with the entrance at the rear.
Traces of red and black lacquer survived on the wood, and several fittings were made of bronze.
The horses wore bronze frontlets and headstalls decorated with cowry shells, and one carried a small bell at the neck.
Bits and cheekpieces were not found in this burial, but both are known from other Anyang chariot burials.

paragraphs.text:
The excavators thought the decoration of M52 and the lack of weapons meant that it was a passenger vehicle rather than a war chariot.
That remains uncertain.
Its construction does not differ fundamentally from chariots found with weapons, and richly decorated vehicles are not thereby excluded from military use.
The sides of the box were too low for stable fighting while standing, so if combat was involved, the riders probably knelt.

paragraphs.text:
Such chariots may therefore have served several purposes.
They could have been ceremonial vehicles for commanders, rapid transport for elite fighters, or moving platforms from which archers shot while kneeling.
The presence of weapons in some burials does not prove that those weapons were meant to be used from a moving vehicle.
In an aristocratic warrior culture, arms also functioned as rank-bearing attire.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

paragraphs.text:
Compared with Near Eastern chariots, those from Anyang are notably large.
They had broader floors, larger wheels, and greater wheel track than the examples from the tomb of Tutankhamun.
That suggests more passengers and less speed.
Yet two features are especially striking.
Anyang wheels can have eighteen to twenty-six spokes, far more than the common four, six, or eight of western Asia, and the axle is mounted not at the back edge of the box but nearer the middle.
Outside China, the closest parallels for this combination are some mid-second-millennium chariots from the Caucasus.

paragraphs.text:
A horse-drawn chariot is not just one more artifact.
It presupposes a whole cluster of skills and institutions.
Horses had to be bred, traded, broken, paired, and handled.
Vehicles had to be built and maintained by skilled carpenters and harness makers.
Drivers too required training.
Even limited use of chariots implies sustained contact with people who already possessed this knowledge.

timeline.label: Chariot arrival at Anyang
timeline.caption: Horse-drawn chariots appear at Anyang already as a complete technical package, implying strong northern contacts.

paragraphs.text:
The logic of the vehicle points the same way.
Speed depends on horse traction, light construction, and spoked wheels.
In western Eurasia such vehicles were preceded by a long history of heavier wagons and draft use.
In China no earlier wheel tradition leads up to the Anyang examples.
The spoked wheel arrives already mature.
Even the heat bending required for some wooden parts presupposes specialized technique.
