timeline.caption: The century in which the short-lived unity of Erligang gives way to clearer regional bronze traditions.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The mature late Shang world centered at Anyang, archaeologically exceptional but no longer treated as the whole of civilization.

timeline.label: Western Zhou
timeline.caption: The later period in which many Bronze Age forms persisted but the old sacrificial and decorative order was reshaped.

timeline.label: Chariot arrival at Anyang
timeline.caption: Horse-drawn chariots appear at Anyang already as a complete technical package, implying strong northern contacts.

paragraphs.text:
The implication is that Anyang did not acquire chariots merely by copying an object seen from afar.
It must have acquired horses, craft knowledge, and at least some trained personnel through northern contacts.
Knives, bow-shaped objects, and other northern-style bronzes appearing in the Anyang record at the same time may belong to that same horizon of interaction.
The arrival of the chariot is therefore one of the clearest signs that the Anyang world was open to forces beyond the Yellow River valley.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Transition period
timeline.caption: The century in which the short-lived unity of Erligang gives way to clearer regional bronze traditions.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The mature late Shang world centered at Anyang, archaeologically exceptional but no longer treated as the whole of civilization.

timeline.label: Western Zhou
timeline.caption: The later period in which many Bronze Age forms persisted but the old sacrificial and decorative order was reshaped.

timeline.label: Chariot arrival at Anyang
timeline.caption: Horse-drawn chariots appear at Anyang already as a complete technical package, implying strong northern contacts.

paragraphs.text:
If one looks beyond Anyang, the most sophisticated contemporary bronze cultures lay in the middle and lower Yangzi region.
By this time, local industries were flourishing across a wide area.
There was no single Anyang horizon imposing one style everywhere.
Instead, the centuries of the Anyang kings saw the confident growth of regional traditions.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Transition period
timeline.caption: The century in which the short-lived unity of Erligang gives way to clearer regional bronze traditions.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The mature late Shang world centered at Anyang, archaeologically exceptional but no longer treated as the whole of civilization.

timeline.label: Western Zhou
timeline.caption: The later period in which many Bronze Age forms persisted but the old sacrificial and decorative order was reshaped.

timeline.label: Xin'gan tomb
timeline.caption: One of the richest transition-period tombs, crucial for the early southern bell tradition and for regional bronze power outside Anyang.

paragraphs.text:
One artifact above all marks the Yangzi sphere.
That is the large bell, especially the type called nao.
Anyang had small bells of this family, but they were minor objects there.
In the south, by contrast, the bell became monumental.
Figure 3.29 makes the contrast immediate.
The Anyang examples are small and relatively modest, whereas southern bells are huge and visually commanding.

figures.label: Figure 3.29
figures.caption: Bells of the nao type from Anyang and the south. The contrast shows how a minor northern object became a monumental ritual form in the Yangzi region.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Transition period
timeline.caption: The century in which the short-lived unity of Erligang gives way to clearer regional bronze traditions.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The mature late Shang world centered at Anyang, archaeologically exceptional but no longer treated as the whole of civilization.

timeline.label: Western Zhou
timeline.caption: The later period in which many Bronze Age forms persisted but the old sacrificial and decorative order was reshaped.

timeline.label: Fu Hao's tomb
timeline.caption: The only intact royal burial at Anyang, exceptionally rich and securely tied to a named figure in the oracle texts.

timeline.label: Xin'gan tomb
timeline.caption: One of the richest transition-period tombs, crucial for the early southern bell tradition and for regional bronze power outside Anyang.

paragraphs.text:
The lower Yangzi seems to have developed this bell tradition first, already by the transition period, and the Xin'gan tomb offers some of the earliest good examples.
In the middle Yangzi region the bells are later and often larger still.
The largest known example, from Ningxiang in Hunan, weighs 220 kilograms.
This is not a marginal craft tradition.
It is the product of courts or centers capable of commanding major resources and directing them toward a ritual ideal very different from that of Anyang.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Transition period
timeline.caption: The century in which the short-lived unity of Erligang gives way to clearer regional bronze traditions.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The mature late Shang world centered at Anyang, archaeologically exceptional but no longer treated as the whole of civilization.

timeline.label: Western Zhou
timeline.caption: The later period in which many Bronze Age forms persisted but the old sacrificial and decorative order was reshaped.

timeline.label: Xin'gan tomb
timeline.caption: One of the richest transition-period tombs, crucial for the early southern bell tradition and for regional bronze power outside Anyang.

timeline.label: Ningxiang great nao
timeline.caption: A monumental bell from Hunan showing how the southern bell tradition became far grander than its northern counterpart.

paragraphs.text:
The middle Yangzi region of southern Hubei and northern Hunan has yielded the greatest concentration of such finds.
Most belong to the period after the transition age, though one great drum from Hubei shows that the southern bronze tradition was already gathering force earlier.
The contexts are often frustratingly poor.
Many objects come from chance finds or deposits rather than well excavated tombs.
Yet the objects themselves are eloquent.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Transition period
timeline.caption: The horizon in which regional bronze traditions emerged after the short-lived unity of Erligang.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The mature late Shang world centered at Anyang, strong but no longer alone in civilized China.

timeline.label: Middle Yangzi bronze world
timeline.caption: The southern Hubei and northern Hunan sphere marked above all by large ritual bells and a narrow but distinctive vessel repertory.

paragraphs.text:
The repertory is narrow but distinctive.
Large bells are the most characteristic items.
After them come imposing zun and lei, and then vessels made in the form of animals such as elephants, buffaloes, and even boars.
Figure 3.30 shows the tone of this world.
The favorite creatures are not the highly formalized imaginary beings that dominate northern bronzes, but real animals rendered with a striking mixture of grandeur and naturalism.

figures.label: Figure 3.30
figures.caption: Animal-shaped bronzes from the middle Yangzi region. What matters is the local preference for powerful real animals rendered with scale, confidence, and natural presence.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Transition period
timeline.caption: The threshold in which some southern forms were already taking shape before the fully mature late Shang age.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The late Shang northern world that provides the main contrast case for the southern bronzes.

timeline.label: Middle Yangzi bronze world
timeline.caption: The regional ritual world whose favored forms were great bells, imposing ding and lei, and animal-shaped bronzes.

paragraphs.text:
Occasionally the southern workshops borrowed something from Anyang, and a few northern imports may have reached the region.
But this does not make the southern bronzes provincial copies.
On the contrary, the scale, technical confidence, and ritual distinctiveness of the finds reveal an independent civilization.
These castings are not the products of simple villages.
If the south still lacks a site in Hunan as thoroughly excavated as Anyang, that is a limitation of modern fieldwork rather than of the ancient culture itself.
The chance finds alone are enough to show that powerful centers with wide connections flourished there, and that the later history of the north, including the rise of Zhou power, cannot be fully understood without them.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Middle Yangzi bronze world
timeline.caption: A southern civilization with its own large-scale casting, ritual priorities, and wide connections.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The late Shang world of the north, useful here mainly as a comparison rather than a standard of superiority.

timeline.label: Zhou conquest
timeline.caption: The later Zhou victory is easier to judge once the strength of the southern bronze world is kept in view.

paragraphs.text:
In 1980 excavations began at Sanxingdui, a walled city on the Chengdu Plain about forty kilometers north of modern Chengdu.
The city wall was a hangtu construction, and the excavators judged it broadly comparable in date to the wall at Zhengzhou, or perhaps even earlier.
The surviving stretches reached as much as six meters in height and forty meters in thickness at the base.
The enclosed area was about 2.6 square kilometers, with settlement debris outside the wall extending over a much larger zone.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Erligang horizon
timeline.caption: The earlier northern horizon whose walls and bronze system provide one point of comparison for Sanxingdui.

timeline.label: Transition period
timeline.caption: The broader late-second-millennium threshold in which regional powers become more visible after Erligang unity weakens.

timeline.label: Sanxingdui horizon
timeline.caption: The main horizon of the great Sanxingdui city and pits on the Chengdu Plain.

timeline.label: Sanxingdui excavations begin
timeline.caption: Modern excavation first established Sanxingdui as a major walled center before the famous pits were found.

timeline.label: Sanxingdui pits discovered
timeline.caption: The pits forced a major rethinking of late second-millennium civilization in Sichuan.

paragraphs.text:
The scale alone shows that Sanxingdui was a major center.
That point was driven home in 1986, when two extraordinary sacrificial pits were found at the site.
The two deposits seem to have been made a few decades apart.
A rough working date places them just before and just after the tomb of Fu Hao, around 1200 BC.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Transition period
timeline.caption: The late-second-millennium horizon in which several regional cultures matured beyond Erligang uniformity.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The late Shang world that overlaps chronologically with the Sanxingdui deposits.

timeline.label: Sanxingdui horizon
timeline.caption: The local Sichuan horizon of the great ritual deposits and their monumental bronzes.

timeline.label: Sanxingdui pit 1 deposit
timeline.caption: One of the two great sacrificial deposits that reveal the ritual world of Sanxingdui.

timeline.label: Sanxingdui pit 2 deposit
timeline.caption: The richer deposit, probably slightly later, that anchors Sanxingdui near the time of Fu Hao.

timeline.label: Fu Hao's tomb
timeline.caption: A major Anyang tomb that provides the nearest familiar chronological landmark.

sections.title: Pit 1 and the Logic of Sacrifice

paragraphs.text:
Pit 1 was a rectangular shaft about 4.6 by 3.5 meters at the top and 1.6 meters deep.
It contained more than three hundred artifacts of bronze, jade, and gold, together with cowry shells, thirteen elephant tusks, and a great mass of burnt animal bone.
No human skeletons were found.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Sanxingdui horizon
timeline.caption: The local horizon of the great ritual deposits on the Chengdu Plain.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The late Shang northern world that serves here mainly as a contrast case.

timeline.label: Sanxingdui pit 1 deposit
timeline.caption: A sacrificial deposit of bronzes, jades, tusks, shells, and burnt remains, unusual in both richness and ritual logic.

paragraphs.text:
The most striking point is ritual rather than quantity.
The artifacts themselves had been burned, and some had been deliberately broken, yet the pit showed no trace of burning.
The most plausible explanation is that animals and objects were first used in a sacrifice elsewhere, then their remains were pushed into the pit and sealed with pounded earth.
Nothing quite like this is known from Anyang, where sacrificial pits usually center on human victims rather than on masses of elite objects.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Sanxingdui horizon
timeline.caption: The horizon of the Sanxingdui pits and their local sacrificial system.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The late Shang world where sacrificial pits usually center more heavily on human victims than on masses of elite objects.

timeline.label: Sanxingdui pit 1 deposit
timeline.caption: The deposit matters because it points to a ritual sequence unlike the better-known sacrificial practices at Anyang.

paragraphs.text:
Pit 2 was similar in form but richer and more orderly.
Its contents were laid down in three levels.
Small items, including more than a hundred jades, lay at the bottom.
Large bronzes occupied the middle layer.
More than sixty elephant tusks formed the uppermost level.
Figure 3.31 shows the excavation clearly.

figures.label: Figure 3.31
figures.caption: Pit 2 at Sanxingdui under excavation. The arrangement of layers helps one see that the deposit was carefully staged, not a random dump.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Transition period
timeline.caption: The wider horizon just before the fully mature late Shang age, relevant because Pit 2 sits close to this threshold.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The late Shang sequence within which Pit 2 is roughly anchored.

timeline.label: Sanxingdui horizon
timeline.caption: The local horizon of the great sacrificial deposits on the Chengdu Plain.

timeline.label: Sanxingdui pit 2 deposit
timeline.caption: The richer and more orderly of the two great ritual deposits at Sanxingdui.

paragraphs.text:
The bronzes from Pit 2 are among the strangest works of the Chinese Bronze Age.
They include a life-size standing figure on a pedestal, shown in Figure 3.32.
They include more than forty bronze heads, some with gold masks and traces of vermilion, and a set of huge faces or masks that border on the monstrous.
Figure 3.33 gives the flavor of these startling forms.

figures.label: Figure 3.32
figures.caption: The life-size bronze statue from Sanxingdui. It shows how far this local bronze tradition moved beyond ordinary vessel casting into an entirely different sacred image world.

figures.label: Figure 3.33
figures.caption: Bronze heads and masks from Sanxingdui. What matters is the radical local imagination of the human and the divine, unlike anything known from Anyang.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Transition period
timeline.caption: The broader threshold at which regional worlds began to diverge more sharply from the Erligang inheritance.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The late Shang northern world that provides the nearest familiar comparison.

timeline.label: Sanxingdui horizon
timeline.caption: The local horizon of the astonishing bronze images from the pits.

timeline.label: Sanxingdui pit 2 deposit
timeline.caption: The deposit preserved the life-size figure, bronze heads, and monumental masks.

timeline.label: Fu Hao's tomb
timeline.caption: A useful chronological anchor because the Sanxingdui pits cluster close to its date while differing radically in ritual imagination.

paragraphs.text:
There were also three bronze trees, the largest over four meters high, found in fragments.
Alongside these stood a dozen large vessels.
Those vessels are in one sense the least surprising objects in the pit, and for that very reason they are archaeologically precious.
They help anchor the dating of a deposit that would otherwise seem almost untethered to anything familiar.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The late Shang sequence provides the nearest familiar dating frame for the Sanxingdui bronzes.

timeline.label: Sanxingdui horizon
timeline.caption: The local horizon in which bronze trees and unusual image forms appear alongside more familiar vessel types.

timeline.label: Sanxingdui pit 2 deposit
timeline.caption: The deposit matters because even its strangest works still sat beside vessels that help place it chronologically.

paragraphs.text:
The vessels from Pit 2 are all zun and lei.
They closely match examples from the middle Yangzi region and may well have been imported from there.
Figure 3.34 makes the resemblance plain.
Metal provenance studies point in the same direction, since some Sanxingdui bronzes appear to share an unusual lead source with bronzes from the Xin'gan tomb.

figures.label: Figure 3.34
figures.caption: A lei from the middle Yangzi region beside one from Sanxingdui. The comparison makes their connection visible while still leaving room for local difference.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Transition period
timeline.caption: The horizon in which regional bronze traditions were already diverging while remaining connected.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The late Shang period that overlaps with both Sanxingdui and the middle Yangzi finds.

timeline.label: Middle Yangzi bronze world
timeline.caption: The regional sphere whose vessel forms and lead sources connect in part with Sanxingdui.

timeline.label: Sanxingdui horizon
timeline.caption: The local Sichuan horizon that remained distinct despite clear lines of contact.

timeline.label: Xin'gan tomb
timeline.caption: A major southern tomb whose bronzes and lead sources help illuminate Sanxingdui's southern connections.

paragraphs.text:
This does not mean that Sanxingdui belonged culturally to the Yangzi world.
The connections are real, but the local culture remains sharply distinct.
The great bells that define the middle Yangzi tradition do not appear here.
Instead there are only a few ling and two clappered bells of highly unusual form.
Sanxingdui was in contact with the south, but it was not merely an outpost of southern taste.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Middle Yangzi bronze world
timeline.caption: The southern bronze world defined above all by large ritual bells and a narrow vessel repertory.

timeline.label: Sanxingdui horizon
timeline.caption: The local Sichuan horizon of the pits and their very different sacred imagery.

timeline.label: Sanxingdui pit 2 deposit
timeline.caption: The deposit matters here because it shows contact with the south without collapse into southern identity.

sections.title: Jades, Technology, and the Problem of Origins

paragraphs.text:
The jades of Sanxingdui are unlike those from Fu Hao's tomb.
Disks and large blades dominate the assemblage.
Arc-ended blades, trapezoidal knives, ge blades, and hybrids between these forms appear in remarkable variety.
Figure 3.35 shows this broad and unruly blade world.
Ornaments, so abundant in Fu Hao's burial, are almost absent here.

figures.label: Figure 3.35
figures.caption: Large jade blades from Sanxingdui. The important point is the extraordinary range of blade forms and the very different jade priorities of this culture.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Transition period
timeline.caption: The threshold at which several regional bronze cultures became clearer in the archaeological record.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The late Shang world whose jade priorities provide the clearest contrast case.

timeline.label: Sanxingdui horizon
timeline.caption: The local horizon in which large blades and unusual jade inventories dominate.

timeline.label: Fu Hao's tomb
timeline.caption: A useful Anyang comparator because its jade assemblage is rich in ornaments rather than massive blades.

paragraphs.text:
The site also poses a historical puzzle.
Pottery suggests some contact with the Erlitou world, and the hangtu wall may reflect influence from Erlitou or Erligang.
Yet the bronzes themselves show little obvious Erligang flavor.
Their casting technology belongs to the Erligang tradition, but their forms and imagery do not.
Perhaps bronze casting reached the Chengdu Plain relatively late, during the transition period rather than during the great Erligang expansion itself.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Erligang horizon
timeline.caption: The earlier horizon of northern expansion whose technology Sanxingdui may have received only indirectly or late.

timeline.label: Transition period
timeline.caption: The likeliest horizon for the transmission of casting into the Chengdu Plain if it did not arrive during the great Erligang spread.

timeline.label: Sanxingdui horizon
timeline.caption: The local horizon whose wall, bronzes, and pottery still resist simple derivation from the north.

paragraphs.text:
The relation of Sanxingdui to later Sichuan history is equally uncertain.
It has often been treated as an ancestor of the later Shu culture, but that continuity has mostly been assumed rather than demonstrated.
Early Western Zhou bronze deposits found nearby seem largely untouched by the strange artistic world of the pits.
Sanxingdui remains one of the great unresolved presences of the second millennium BC.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Sanxingdui horizon
timeline.caption: The horizon of the strange Sichuan pits, still difficult to place securely in longer regional history.

timeline.label: Early Western Zhou
timeline.caption: The period whose nearby deposits show surprisingly little continuation of the Sanxingdui image world.

timeline.label: Zhou conquest
timeline.caption: A useful threshold after which the odd artistic world of the pits becomes much harder to trace.

paragraphs.text:
Shandong presents a different problem.
At Sufutun, near modern Qingzhou, bronzes were first found in 1931 in a small hill at the edge of the village.
Later work confirmed that the hill contained a cemetery, though no associated habitation site was securely identified nearby.
A chariot burial and four tombs were excavated in 1965 and 1966, and six more tombs in 1986.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Late Anyang phase
timeline.caption: The later part of the Anyang sequence to which the excavated Sufutun tombs belong.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The broader late Shang field against which Sufutun must be measured.

timeline.label: Sufutun cemetery
timeline.caption: The Shandong cemetery that reveals a powerful eastern extension of the late Shang world.

paragraphs.text:
All the excavated tombs are assigned to the later part of the Anyang sequence, roughly Yinxu 3 or 4.
Most had been plundered before excavation.
Even so, three tombs are revealing.
Two smaller burials, M7 and M8, survived intact enough to show their contents, while the great tomb M1 remained important because of its scale and sacrificial wealth.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Late Anyang phase
timeline.caption: The later Yinxu horizon relevant for the excavated Sufutun burials.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The broader late Shang world whose burial forms and bronzes shape the comparison.

timeline.label: Sufutun M1
timeline.caption: The great tomb at Sufutun, important for its combination of Anyang-like form and heavy sacrifice.

timeline.label: Sufutun M7 and M8
timeline.caption: The smaller but informative tombs that clarify the local character of the cemetery.

paragraphs.text:
M7 was a rectangular shaft with a yaokeng containing a dog and an ercengtai on which three sacrificed humans had been laid, one of them a child.
Most of the grave goods were placed between inner and outer coffins.
They included eight bronze vessels, seven weapons, and bronzes inscribed with the emblem conventionally read as Ya Chou.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Late Anyang phase
timeline.caption: The later Yinxu horizon of the Sufutun burials.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The broader late Shang world from which most of the burial form derives.

timeline.label: Sufutun M7
timeline.caption: A smaller Shandong tomb whose form and goods show close links with the late Anyang world.

paragraphs.text:
M8 was somewhat larger and had a sloping southern ramp ten meters long.
It did not contain sacrificed humans, but dogs appeared in the upper part of the tomb and animal remains lay in the yaokeng below.
The grave goods included seventeen bronze vessels, three nao, eight ling, a stone chime, more than two hundred arrowheads, two axes, and about thirty bronze weapons.
The excavators thought many of the weapons were made cheaply for burial rather than for serious use.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Late Anyang phase
timeline.caption: The later Anyang sequence to which M8 belongs.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The broader late Shang ritual world, present here but locally inflected.

timeline.label: Sufutun M8
timeline.caption: A larger tomb whose bells, chime, and mass of weapons sharpen the profile of the Sufutun cemetery.

paragraphs.text:
These smaller tombs already show something unusual about Sufutun.
The burial form is largely familiar from the Anyang world, yet the assemblages also have their own local emphases.
The emblem Ya Chou links some of the bronzes to a broader group of objects now scattered in collections without clear provenance.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Late Anyang phase
timeline.caption: The later Yinxu horizon shared by the Sufutun burials.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The late Shang field whose forms remain dominant even in eastern Shandong.

timeline.label: Ya Chou emblem
timeline.caption: The emblem links some Sufutun bronzes to a wider late Shang group that was once difficult to place.

sections.title: Tomb M1 and the Scale of Sacrifice

paragraphs.text:
M1 was the dominant tomb at the cemetery.
It had four ramps and a cross-shaped wooden burial chamber, making it the only tomb outside the Xibeigang cemetery that clearly follows the great royal pattern of Anyang.
The south ramp ran all the way down to the floor of the shaft.
The other ramps entered higher, at the level of the ercengtai.
Though much smaller than M1001 at Xibeigang, it is the largest tomb yet known outside Anyang.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Late Anyang phase
timeline.caption: The later Yinxu horizon to which the great Sufutun tomb belongs.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The broader late Shang world whose royal mortuary pattern M1 partly imitates.

timeline.label: Sufutun M1
timeline.caption: The largest tomb known outside Anyang that still clearly follows the great royal pattern.

timeline.label: Xibeigang M1001
timeline.caption: The major Anyang comparison for the cross-shaped chamber and ramped tomb form.

paragraphs.text:
The sacrifices were immense.
Forty-eight human victims were recorded altogether, most of them teenagers or younger.
Two were buried in pits in the chamber floor, one with a dog.
Seven more lay on the ercengtai, apparently in coffins.
The rest, whether whole bodies or severed heads, were deposited in three layers in the south ramp together with five dogs.
Even after looting, the tomb still yielded two large bronze axes, one inscribed with the Ya Chou emblem, along with jades, gold foil, and thousands of cowries.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Late Anyang phase
timeline.caption: The later Yinxu horizon in which the Sufutun sacrifices must be set.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The broader late Shang mortuary world in which heavy sacrifice remained a sign of extreme rank.

timeline.label: Sufutun M1
timeline.caption: The Shandong tomb is important because its sacrificial scale approaches the great Anyang cemetery more than any other outlying site.

paragraphs.text:
Several other features are suggestive.
Some tombs at Sufutun had ramps, something otherwise known mainly from the great royal tombs at Anyang.
M1 had charcoal spread beneath the wooden outer coffin.
M8 had the lower walls lined with bundles of reeds.
Another large tomb, M2, placed a ge, a shield, and a human head in each corner of the shaft.
These details do not amount to a wholly separate local tradition, but they give the cemetery its own character.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Late Anyang phase
timeline.caption: The later Anyang horizon relevant to M1, M2, and the associated eastern cemetery forms.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The larger late Shang field from which many of the burial features derive.

timeline.label: Sufutun M1
timeline.caption: The tomb remains the main eastern case for a strongly Anyang-like but locally marked elite burial.

paragraphs.text:
The Ya Chou emblem occurs on bronzes from M1 and M7, on an earlier bronze found at Sufutun in 1931, and on several spearheads said to come from the same site.
It is also known from many unprovenanced bronzes.
Because the emblem does not seem securely tied to other excavated sites, it is tempting to connect much of that unprovenanced material with Sufutun.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Late Anyang phase
timeline.caption: The later Yinxu horizon in which the Ya Chou bronzes are best situated.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The broader late Shang world to which the Sufutun bronzes remain closely tied.

timeline.label: Ya Chou emblem
timeline.caption: The emblem anchors a scattered group of bronzes that likely connect back to Sufutun.

paragraphs.text:
The published Ya Chou bronzes number in the dozens.
They include many large and flamboyant castings, with an unusually high proportion of rectangular vessels.
Some of the square-bodied jue are among the most eccentric bronzes of the late Shang world.
For a long time they seemed almost without parallel at Anyang itself.
But a tomb found at Anyang in 1990 yielded very similar pieces.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Late Anyang phase
timeline.caption: The later Anyang horizon within which the flamboyant Ya Chou repertoire belongs.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The broader late Shang field whose style these bronzes push toward extremes rather than leave behind.

timeline.label: Anyang tomb with similar Ya Chou pieces
timeline.caption: The modern find mattered because it showed that the Ya Chou forms were not alien to the Anyang repertoire after all.

paragraphs.text:
This matters for interpretation.
The Ya Chou bronzes are not foreign to the Anyang style, even when they push it toward theatrical excess.
They belong within its range.
That makes Sufutun look less like a wholly independent culture than like a place tied quite closely to the late Anyang sphere.
On present evidence, Shandong here looks more like a late eastern extension of that civilization than like a fully separate regional power.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Late Anyang phase
timeline.caption: The relevant horizon for Sufutun and its emblem-bearing bronzes.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The late Shang field within which Sufutun now looks better placed.

timeline.label: Ya Chou emblem
timeline.caption: The emblem is useful because it ties an eastern group of bronzes back into the late Anyang sphere.

paragraphs.text:
A very different world stretched across the north.
The Northern Zone ran from Xinjiang and Gansu in the west to Jilin in the east, including much of Inner Mongolia and parts of Liaoning, Hebei, Shanxi, and northern Shaanxi.
In the first millennium BC this broad region would become the home of pastoral nomadism.
But even in the late second millennium BC it already housed cultures visibly distinct from the civilization of the middle Yellow River valley.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Erligang horizon
timeline.caption: The earlier northern horizon whose frontier retreat forms part of the background to the Northern Zone problem.

timeline.label: Late second-millennium Northern Zone
timeline.caption: The broad northern field of bronze-using cultures distinct from the middle Yellow River world.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The late Shang world that dealt directly with northern peoples and artifacts.

paragraphs.text:
Those cultures matter for at least two reasons.
First, their bronzes point to a metalworking tradition independent of the Erligang world, with roots reaching northward and westward into Siberia and Central Asia.
Second, the spread of their characteristic inventory seems roughly to coincide with the southward retreat of the Erligang culture from some frontier zones.
The appearance of the Northern Complex may therefore signal not just trade, but movement of peoples and a reshaping of the political frontier.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Erligang horizon
timeline.caption: The earlier northern horizon against which the retreat from some frontier zones becomes visible.

timeline.label: Transition period
timeline.caption: The threshold during which frontier reorganization and new regional powers become harder to miss.

timeline.label: Late second-millennium Northern Zone
timeline.caption: The horizon of northern bronzes that looks independent of the Erligang tradition.

paragraphs.text:
The Anyang court clearly had dealings with this world.
Northern-style knives, mirrors, and bow-shaped objects appear in Anyang burials, and the arrival of the chariot belongs to the same broad horizon of contact.
At the same time, bronze vessels of the central tradition moved northward into the zone.
The frontier was not a wall.
It was a zone of transmission, borrowing, and transformation.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Late second-millennium Northern Zone
timeline.caption: The northern bronze world with roots reaching far beyond China proper.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The late Shang courtly world that borrowed from and sent objects into the north.

timeline.label: Arrival of the chariot at Anyang
timeline.caption: A major sign that northern contact was reshaping elite practices at Anyang.

paragraphs.text:
The bronzes of the Northern Complex are distinctive both in type and in decoration.
Simple striations and zigzags are common.
So are loops, buttons for suspension, and terminals shaped like rattles or animal heads.
Characteristic types include mirrors, battle-axes with tubular sockets, knives cast in one piece with their handles, daggers, spoons, spatulas, and ladles.
Figures 3.36 and 3.37 show several of these forms.

figures.label: Figure 3.36
figures.caption: Axes from Panlongcheng, Xin'gan, and the Northern Zone. The comparison helps clarify how different hafting methods reveal different technical traditions.

figures.label: Figure 3.37
figures.caption: Knives and daggers from Anyang and the north. These forms show at a glance how clearly northern weapons stand apart from native central styles.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Late second-millennium Northern Zone
timeline.caption: The horizon of bronzes whose forms and decoration mark an independent northern lineage.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The central late Shang world that provides the clearest comparison for northern types.

timeline.label: Early Western Zhou
timeline.caption: The later period in which some northern forms continued to move into frontier burials and mixed assemblages.

paragraphs.text:
These types are immediately recognizable beside the native bronzes of the Yellow River world.
Chinese axes normally have flat tangs for hafting.
Northern ones often use shaft rings or tubular sockets.
Chinese knives usually have separate handles.
Northern knives cast blade and handle together.
The differences are not minor.
They point to different technical lineages.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Late second-millennium Northern Zone
timeline.caption: The horizon in which mirrors, socketed axes, integrated knives, and daggers define a different technical inheritance.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The late Shang world whose native bronze types sharply differ from those of the north.

timeline.label: Early Western Zhou
timeline.caption: The comparison matters partly because later frontier burials continued to mix these lineages.

paragraphs.text:
Some northern borrowings were naturalized in China and others were not.
Ge blades with shaft rings seem to represent a temporary experiment inspired by northern hafting methods.
Ring-handled knives, by contrast, became a permanent acquisition and eventually displaced the older native type.
The northern bronzes therefore matter not only as markers of ethnic or regional difference, but also as sources of technical change within China itself.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Late second-millennium Northern Zone
timeline.caption: The northern horizon whose tools and weapons sometimes entered China proper.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The late Shang world that adopted some northern forms while ignoring others.

timeline.label: Arrival of the chariot at Anyang
timeline.caption: The wider horizon of northern borrowings helps explain why some object types were naturalized in China.

paragraphs.text:
The archaeology of Shanxi shows how complex this frontier world could be.
A small grave at Jixian contained only four bronzes, all of clearly northern character.
A battle-axe and a dagger lay by the head of the dead, and two large spoons lay beside the hand.
Figure 3.38 shows the arrangement.
Here there is little sign of influence from Anyang.

figures.label: Figure 3.38
figures.caption: A grave at Jixian in Shanxi. The burial is useful because it presents a compact assemblage of distinctly northern weapons and utensils without obvious Anyang features.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Late second-millennium Northern Zone
timeline.caption: The northern horizon whose artifact types remain dominant in some Shanxi burials.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The late Shang comparison case, useful precisely because it is weak in this small grave.

timeline.label: Jixian grave
timeline.caption: A compact Shanxi burial whose contents lean strongly toward northern rather than Anyang forms.

paragraphs.text:
At Lingshi, however, two large graves contained only a few northern items alongside rich groups of Anyang-style vessels.
The burials also had familiar features such as yaokeng with sacrificed dogs.
In these graves the northern pieces look like intrusions into an otherwise central style of aristocratic burial.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Late second-millennium Northern Zone
timeline.caption: The northern horizon that continued to furnish some items even in mixed elite burials.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The late Shang mortuary and vessel tradition that remains dominant in the richer Lingshi graves.

timeline.label: Lingshi graves
timeline.caption: The mixed graves show northern items entering an otherwise central style of aristocratic burial.

paragraphs.text:
At Shilou the balance shifts again.
There, small northern bronzes repeatedly occur with vessels so eccentric that they must be local products.
Figure 3.39 gives some idea of this mixed repertoire, with ornaments, weapons, utensils, and an extraordinary boat-shaped vessel all appearing together.
The implication is not simple diffusion in one direction.
Different groups, economies, and habits of burial were overlapping in the same broad region.

figures.label: Figure 3.39
figures.caption: Objects from several finds at Shilou in Shanxi. Taken together, they show a frontier world where ornaments, weapons, utensils, and odd local vessels overlap.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Late second-millennium Northern Zone
timeline.caption: The northern horizon contributes many small bronzes to the Shanxi frontier world.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The late Shang comparison case against which the local eccentricity of Shilou stands out.

timeline.label: Shilou finds
timeline.caption: The Shilou group matters because it shows overlap, not simple one-way diffusion, on the Shanxi frontier.

sections.title: The Wei Valley before the Zhou

paragraphs.text:
The Wei valley has often been mined archaeologically in hopes of finding the early Zhou.
The search usually aims to identify a pottery assemblage that can be labeled proto-Zhou.
But the whole enterprise is uncertain.
Later texts are too vague and contradictory to fix the homeland securely, and there is no good reason to expect pottery styles to map neatly onto peoples named in literature.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Late second-millennium Wei valley
timeline.caption: The mixed regional field out of which the Zhou would later emerge.

timeline.label: Early Western Zhou
timeline.caption: The later period that shaped the backward search for a proto-Zhou homeland.

timeline.label: Zhou conquest
timeline.caption: The political threshold that later encouraged archaeologists to mine the Wei valley for origins.

paragraphs.text:
What does stand out clearly is cultural mixture.
Erligang bronzes reached deep into the valley.
The eastern end, around places such as Laoniupo near Xi'an, shows strong ties with Anyang and also with the Northern Zone.
Farther north, as one moves toward Chunhua, Tongchuan, Suide, and Qingjian, northern bronzes appear with increasing frequency alongside vessel types that remain linked to the central tradition.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Erligang horizon
timeline.caption: The earlier horizon whose bronzes reached deeply into the Wei valley.

timeline.label: Late second-millennium Northern Zone
timeline.caption: The northern horizon that increasingly overlaps with central forms in the upper valley and frontier districts.

timeline.label: Late second-millennium Wei valley
timeline.caption: A mixed field of borrowing, overlap, and recombination rather than a clean proto-Zhou package.

timeline.label: Laoniupo
timeline.caption: The eastern Wei valley site shows ties both to Anyang and to the Northern Zone.

paragraphs.text:
The valley was also open to the south.
Objects from Laoniupo and Chenggu connect the Wei world with southern Shaanxi and even with Xin'gan in Jiangxi.
Chenggu may have served as an intermediary between the Yangzi sphere and the Wei valley.
Traffic moving along these routes helps explain why southern influence on early Zhou bronzes later became so strong.
The valley that produced the Zhou was not culturally isolated.
It was a region of receiving, borrowing, and recombining.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Late second-millennium Wei valley
timeline.caption: The mixed world out of which Zhou power later emerged.

timeline.label: Middle Yangzi bronze world
timeline.caption: A southern sphere whose influence likely reached north through intermediary routes.

timeline.label: Chenggu
timeline.caption: A likely intermediary linking the Wei valley with southern Shaanxi and farther southern bronze worlds.

timeline.label: Xin'gan tomb
timeline.caption: Its distant connection helps show how widely linked the Wei valley already was before the Zhou conquest.

paragraphs.text:
Archaeology makes one point especially difficult to ignore.
The Zhou did not emerge from the most materially sophisticated part of the late second-millennium world.
The middle Yangzi region seems in many respects more advanced in bronze casting and more exuberant in large ritual display.
Anyang itself was richer and more textually visible.
The preconquest Wei valley, by contrast, appears mixed, receptive, and comparatively rough.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Middle Yangzi bronze world
timeline.caption: A southern sphere whose large ritual bronzes may have surpassed the preconquest Zhou world in material splendor.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The late Shang courtly world, richer and more textually visible than the preconquest Wei valley.

timeline.label: Late second-millennium Wei valley
timeline.caption: The rougher and more mixed regional world from which the Zhou emerged.

paragraphs.text:
This gives the Zhou conquest a different texture.
The Anyang dynasty was not overthrown by the most civilized of its contemporaries, but perhaps by a neighbor especially eager to acquire civilization.
That possibility helps explain why the early Zhou world could be at once politically triumphant and culturally indebted.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Late second-millennium Wei valley
timeline.caption: The mixed frontier field that formed the immediate background of the Zhou.

timeline.label: Early Western Zhou
timeline.caption: The period in which political triumph and cultural indebtedness were both visible.

timeline.label: Zhou conquest
timeline.caption: The conquest matters because archaeology makes it look less like the victory of the most civilized power and more like the triumph of a receptive frontier neighbor.

paragraphs.text:
Taken as a whole, the archaeological record points to a second millennium far wider and more varied than traditional history allows.
There were civilized centers in the Chengdu Plain, in the Yangzi valley, in Shandong, and beyond, alongside simpler but still significant bronze-using societies in the north and northwest.
The world of late Shang was not a single cultural block ruled unambiguously from one center.
It was a large, diverse, and interacting field.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Late second millennium BC
timeline.caption: The broad field within which several civilized centers coexisted across China.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The late Shang courtly world remains central, but only as one major node among others.

timeline.label: Early Western Zhou
timeline.caption: The later period whose political memory narrowed the picture of the preceding age.

paragraphs.text:
Traditional history remembers something much narrower.
It presents an ancient China in which the only fully civilized powers were Shang and Zhou, and in which the decisive event was the lawful transfer of universal rule from one dynasty to the next.
That account was shaped by the Zhou themselves.
Its earliest formulations belong to texts concerned with political legitimation and the doctrine later called the Mandate of Heaven.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The late Shang world that later texts elevated into the single civilized center.

timeline.label: Early Western Zhou
timeline.caption: The period in which the doctrine of rightful dynastic transfer was articulated and projected backward.

timeline.label: Zhou conquest
timeline.caption: The political break that later tradition recast as a lawful transfer of universal rule.

paragraphs.text:
From that point of view, the omissions begin to make sense.
To justify their conquest, the Zhou needed a past in which they had replaced not one power among many, but the single rightful ruler of the civilized world.
That required assigning a unique status to the kings of Anyang and inventing, behind them, an earlier precedent for lawful dynastic transfer.
Archaeology does not simply add detail to that story.
It destabilizes its whole framework.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Anyang milieu
timeline.caption: The late Shang courtly world whose memory was reshaped by later Zhou political needs.

timeline.label: Early Western Zhou
timeline.caption: The period in which the narrow traditional framework for pre-Zhou history was formed.

timeline.label: Mandate of Heaven formulation
timeline.caption: The ideological move mattered because it helped turn one conquest among many powers into a moralized dynastic succession.

paragraphs.text:
The value of archaeology therefore lies not only in recovering forgotten places.
It also lies in showing how power reshapes memory.
As long as archaeology is used chiefly to vindicate tradition, the second millennium will remain distorted.
Once it is allowed to stand independently, both the pre-Zhou world and the Zhou invention of history become far more interesting.

figures.label: Map 3.1
figures.caption: A map locating Anyang and the region from which the oracle bones entered modern scholarship. It matters because it ties the textual discovery to a real archaeological landscape.

timeline.label: Late second millennium BC
timeline.caption: The archaeological field recovered by this chapter once later dynastic simplifications are set aside.

timeline.label: Early Western Zhou
timeline.caption: The period that reshaped memory and narrowed the traditional narrative of the age before it.

timeline.label: Zhou conquest
timeline.caption: The threshold after which political power began to reorganize the remembered past as well as the living world.
